result:
The first thing I remember wanting to be was a paleontologist. Not just any paleontologist, but the kind who discovers an entirely new species, something with a name that rolls off the tongue like a spell, something that forces textbooks to be rewritten. I was seven, and my bedroom floor was a graveyard of plastic dinosaurs, their rigid bodies arranged in elaborate dioramas on a braided rug that smelled faintly of dust and crayons. I understood, even then, that these creatures were gone, that the world they inhabited was not my world, but I felt a strange kinship with them—these monumental failures of adaptation, these proofs that existence is not a ladder but a chaotic dance where the music stops at random intervals. The past, I believed, was a puzzle that could be solved if you just dug deep enough, brushed away enough sand, held enough fragments of bone up to the light.

That certainty faded, as certainties do. The dinosaurs gave way to an obsession with space, then with maps, then with the inner cartography of sentences and paragraphs. But the impulse remained the same: a desire to excavate, to unearth something buried, to trace the outline of a hidden shape. I think that’s what writing is, at its core. You’re not inventing so much as uncovering, taking a brush to the compacted layers of memory and experience and hoping a form emerges that someone else might recognize. The problem, of course, is that the fossil record of the self is notoriously unreliable. The bones shift when you’re not looking. The strata get scrambled. You dig up what you think is a femur and it turns out to be a wishbone, a stalactite, a piece of the neighbor’s fence that got sucked into a tornado twenty years ago.

I am writing this now in a room that is not my own, in a city that is not my own, at a time that feels borrowed. The light through the window is the flat, white glare of a screen’s backlight, even though I’m looking at a tree. A maple, I think. Its leaves are green now, but in a month they’ll start to turn, and I’ll have moved on by then. This is the condition of modern life: perpetual motion, temporary roots. We are a generation of potted plants, convinced we can thrive as long as we get enough Wi-Fi and occasional validation. I’m not complaining. I’m observing. There’s a difference, though it’s getting harder to tell.

The tree outside my window doesn’t care about any of this. It’s been there for decades, pushing its roots down into the dark, drinking whatever the earth offers. It doesn’t worry about its legacy or its carbon footprint or whether it’s said the wrong thing on social media. It simply is, with a completeness that I both envy and find a little terrifying. I’ve spent so much of my life trying to be something—a good student, a good writer, a good person—that I’ve rarely paused to simply be. Meditation apps tell me to focus on my breath, to exist in the present moment, but the present moment keeps getting interrupted by notifications. Even now, a part of my brain is wondering if I should check my email. There is no reason to check my email. Nothing important will have arrived in the last eight minutes. And yet the pull is there, a small magnetic tug toward the digital nowhere, the infinite scroll, the curated lives of people I haven’t spoken to since high school.

I think about the dinosaurs again. They had no concept of distraction, no sense of a self that needed constant maintenance. They were too busy surviving, and then they weren’t. Extinction is a hard stop, but it’s also a kind of purification. The asteroid didn’t care about their social status or their unread messages. It just erased the board and let a new game begin. There’s something almost comforting about that scale of indifference. The universe, for all its grandeur, is not judgmental. It doesn’t hold grudges. It doesn’t keep a tally of your failures. It just moves on, rearranging atoms into new configurations, turning stardust into saplings, saplings into coal, coal into diamonds, diamonds into symbols we put on rings and fight wars over.

I’ve been trying to read more poetry lately. Not because I think it will make me a better writer—though that might be a side effect—but because poetry operates on a logic that is not the logic of algorithms. It doesn’t optimize. It doesn’t streamline. It meanders, loops back, makes unexpected leaps. A good poem is like a crack in the pavement where a weed has pushed through, a small rebellion against the flattening of experience. The internet, for all its wonders, flattens. It takes the messy, three-dimensional texture of being alive and compresses it into a two-dimensional scroll, a highlight reel, a series of hot takes and viral moments that dissolve as quickly as they appear. I’m part of this system. I contribute to it. I’m not throwing stones from outside the glass house; I’m standing inside it, tapping on the pane, wondering if anyone can hear the difference between a tap and a crack.

When I was in college, I took a class on the philosophy of time. We read Augustine and Bergson and McTaggart, and I understood almost none of it. But one idea lodged itself in me like a splinter: the notion that the present is all we really have, and that the past and future are mental constructs. The past is memory, which is notoriously fallible. The future is anticipation, which is often anxiety in disguise. The present is the only thing that’s real, but it slips through our fingers the moment we try to grasp it. We are always a little behind ourselves, processing the last moment while the next one barrels toward us. This is why I hate having my photo taken. A photograph fixes you in a moment that’s already dead, and asks you to perform for a future you can’t even imagine. I always look slightly startled in photos, like a small mammal caught in a sudden light.

But I’m digressing. That’s allowed here. This is free writing, after all. No structure, no thesis, no neat conclusion waiting at the end like a prize in a cereal box. Just the movement of thought, the way one idea snags on another, the way memory bubbles up unbidden. Like right now, I’m suddenly remembering a road trip I took with my father when I was twelve. We drove from Ohio to Florida in a minivan that smelled of sunscreen and old coffee. I had a Discman and a binder full of CDs—Nirvana, Green Day, Alanis Morissette—and I spent most of the trip with headphones on, staring out the window at the passing strip malls and pine forests. My father tried to talk to me, but I was at that age where every conversation felt like a trap. What do you want to be when you grow up? What are you learning in school? Do you have any friends who aren’t made of pixels on a screen? (This was the late nineties; pixels were still an accusation.) I gave him monosyllabic answers and turned the volume up. Now my father is older, and I live thousands of miles away, and I would give anything to have that lost time back, to tell him about the dinosaurs and the poems and the philosophy of the present moment. But the time is gone. The road trip is a fossil in my mind, a fragment of bone that I polish until it shines, hoping it will reveal something about who I am.

The writer in me knows that this is material. That’s a cynical word, material. It turns experience into a resource, something to be mined, refined, and sold. But I don’t mean it cynically. I mean that we are all making a narrative out of our lives, consciously or not. The brain is a storytelling machine. It takes the chaotic input of the senses and weaves it into a coherent timeline, complete with protagonists and antagonists, rising action and denouement. The problem is that life doesn’t actually have a plot. It has patterns, yes, and cause and effect, but no author sits down and says, “And then, in a surprising twist, Sarah loses her job and discovers her true passion for pottery.” The twists come, but they’re random, or at least they feel random when you’re inside them. It’s only in retrospect that we impose a shape, and that shape is always a lie, or at best a metaphor.

I’m thinking about metaphors, how they are the fundamental currency of thought. We can’t understand anything without comparing it to something else. Time is a river. Love is a battlefield. The internet is a web, a cloud, a series of tubes. We use these images to make the abstract graspable, but they also constrain our thinking. When we say time is a river, we imply it flows in one direction, at a constant speed, toward an endpoint. But time doesn’t do that. Time pools and eddies and sometimes seems to run uphill. A minute in the dentist’s chair is not the same duration as a minute in the arms of someone you love. Einstein knew this. So did Proust. So does anyone who has ever waited for test results or a text back from a person who is slowly losing interest.

The tree outside my window is still a tree. It hasn’t become a metaphor yet, though I’m sure I could force it. The tree as a symbol of rootedness, of patience, of the natural world that exists in defiance of the digital. But that feels too easy. The tree is just a tree, doing its tree things, and my desire to make it mean something is part of the human condition, the same impulse that made me arrange plastic dinosaurs on a rug. We are pattern-seekers, meaning-makers, forever trying to connect the dots. And when there are no dots, we draw them ourselves.

I had a conversation recently with a friend about artificial intelligence. He’s terrified of it; I’m cautiously intrigued. He sees the end of creativity, the death of the soul, the rise of a machine that can mimic us so perfectly that we become obsolete. I see another tool, one that might force us to rethink what we mean by “creativity” in the first place. If a large language model can write a poem that moves you, is the poem any less valuable? Does the value lie in the intention of the creator, or in the response of the audience? If a tree falls in the forest and no one is around to hear it, it makes a sound—sound is a physical phenomenon—but it doesn’t make a meaning. Meaning is a collaborative act. It requires a mind to receive it. And if that mind is moved by a string of words generated by an algorithm, then meaning has occurred, regardless of the source. I’m not sure I fully believe this, but it’s a useful provocation. It forces me to consider what I do when I write. Am I just a more complex algorithm, one trained on a dataset of my own experiences? Or is there something else, a ghost in the machine, a spark that can’t be reduced to code?

I don’t know the answer, and I’m comfortable with that. Certainty is overrated. The older I get, the more I realize that the people who speak with absolute confidence are usually the ones who have stopped thinking. Doubt is the engine of inquiry, the grit that produces the pearl. I want to live in the space between knowing and not-knowing, in the fertile pause where new ideas gestate. This is another reason I read poetry. A good poem doesn’t tell you what to think; it invites you to join an act of discovery. It’s an open question, not a closed argument. And in a world that bombards us with answers—often to questions we never asked—that openness feels like oxygen.

I’m running out of steam, not because I have nothing left to say, but because the act of writing is exhausting in a way that’s hard to explain. It’s not just the physical effort of typing, though my wrists are starting to ache. It’s the emotional exposure, the feeling of being peeled back one layer at a time, even when you’re writing about dinosaurs and trees. Every sentence is a small confession. Every paragraph is a choice about what to reveal and what to conceal. The blank page is a mirror, and you don’t always like the face staring back.

But I committed to 2500 words, so I’ll keep going. That’s another thing about writing: it’s a discipline as much as an art. The muse doesn’t show up until you do. You have to sit in the chair, open the document, and start typing, even if what comes out feels like sludge. Anne Lamott calls it the “shitty first draft.” I’ve learned to trust the process, to know that the sludge is just the top layer, and that if I keep digging, I might hit something solid. Or I might not. That’s the risk. That’s the thrill.

I think about the paleontologist again. The hours spent in the sun, the careful brushing, the excitement of a shape emerging from the rock. Most of the time, it’s just a rock. But occasionally, it’s a bone. And that bone connects to another bone, and that bone to a whole skeleton, and suddenly you have a creature that no one has seen for millions of years. Writing is like that. You sift through the debris of your own consciousness, and most of it is junk—half-remembered song lyrics, grocery lists, grudges, fantasies, the smell of sunscreen in a minivan. But sometimes, if you’re patient, you find a fragment that fits with another fragment, and a structure starts to reveal itself. It might not be a dinosaur. It might be a sentence, a scene, a character who walks off the page and refuses to leave. And for a moment, you feel like you’re not building something so much as recovering it, like it was always there, waiting for someone to come along and notice.

This morning I watched a squirrel run along the branch of the maple tree. It moved with an agility that seemed impossible, leaping from twig to twig without any apparent fear of falling. I thought, That squirrel is not thinking about the future. It’s not worrying about its retirement account or whether it said the wrong thing to the squirrel from the next yard. It’s just moving, moment to moment, guided by instinct and hunger and the imperative to survive. I’m not advocating for a return to pure instinct—that way lies chaos—but I do think there’s something to learn from the squirrel’s total absorption in the present task. When I’m writing, really writing, I experience a version of that absorption. Time disappears. The ego dissolves. I’m not a person with a history and a future; I’m just a channel, a conduit, a node in a network of language and image. That state is the closest I’ve come to a spiritual experience. It’s not about belief; it’s about surrender.

And yet, I can’t sustain it. The self comes roaring back, demanding attention. Did I pay the electric bill? Is my posture okay? Am I wasting my life pursuing something that will never yield a stable income? The tree outside my window is a maple, but it’s also a clock, its leaves charting the passage of the season. Soon they’ll turn orange, then brown, then fall. Then snow, then buds, then green again. The cycle continues, indifferent to my anxieties. The squirrel will stash its nuts. The tree will send its roots deeper. And I’ll be somewhere else, looking out a different window, still trying to dig up the bones of a story that won’t stay buried.

I’ve been avoiding the topic of climate change, but it’s impossible to ignore. The dinosaurs were wiped out by an asteroid; we’re doing it to ourselves, slowly, with carbon and consumption and a collective refusal to see what’s right in front of us. I’m complicit. We all are. I fly on airplanes. I eat food wrapped in plastic. I type on a laptop made from rare earth minerals mined in conditions I’d rather not imagine. The weight of it presses down on me, and I do what most of us do: I scroll past the headlines, I donate to a cause, I try not to think about the world my hypothetical grandchildren will inherit. It’s not enough. It’s never enough. And yet, what else can a single person do? The problem is structural, systemic, woven into the fabric of modern existence. Individual action feels like trying to bail out the ocean with a teaspoon.

This is the moment where a personal essay is supposed to pivot toward hope, offer a solution, end on a note of uplift. But I don’t have a solution, and I’m not sure hope is what we need. Hope can be a way of deferring action, a reassurance that everything will work out in the end. I’m more interested in resilience, in the ability to face the mess without flinching, to do the work even when the outcome is uncertain. The tree outside my window is resilient. It’s survived storms and droughts and the exhaust fumes of a thousand cars. It doesn’t need hope. It just keeps growing.

I’m nearing the end of my 2500 words. I can feel the sentences starting to circle back, looking for a landing place. The light has shifted; the afternoon has arrived. The tree is still there. The squirrel has moved on to some other branch, some other moment. I’m still here, tapping at a keyboard, trying to put something into the world that wasn’t there before. I don’t know if I’ve succeeded. I don’t know if I’ve said anything true. But I’ve written, and that counts for something. In a culture of consumption, creation is a radical act. It’s a refusal to be passive, a declaration that your perspective matters, even if no one else ever reads it.

The dinosaurs are still gone. The plastic versions of them are probably in a landfill, or a box in my parents’ basement, or the bottom of a thrift store bin. But the part of me that loved them—the part that still loves them—is here, translated into language, preserved in the amber of a digital document. That’s the closest we come to immortality. Not fame, not legacy, but the small, persistent act of making something, of leaving a mark, however faint, on the surface of the world. The asteroid is always coming. That’s not pessimism; it’s just geology. The question is what we do with the time between now and impact. I say we dig. I say we write. I say we pay attention to the squirrel, and the tree, and the flat white light of a screen that, for all its limitations, still allows us to reach out across the emptiness and say, I was here. I saw this. I felt this. Does anyone else feel it too?

The dinosaurs were wiped out by an asteroid; we’re doing it to ourselves, slowly, with carbon and consumption and a collective refusal to see what’s right in front of us. I’m complicit. We all are. I fly on airplanes. I eat food wrapped in plastic. I type on a laptop made from rare earth minerals mined in conditions I’d rather not imagine. The weight of it presses down on me, and I do what most of us do: I scroll past the headlines, I donate to a cause, I try not to think about the world my hypothetical grandchildren will inherit. It’s not enough. It’s never enough. And yet, what else can a single person do? The problem is structural, systemic, woven into the fabric of modern existence. Individual action feels like trying to bail out the ocean with a teaspoon.

This is the moment where a personal essay is supposed to pivot toward hope, offer a solution, end on a note of uplift. But I don’t have a solution, and I’m not sure hope is what we need. Hope can be a way of deferring action, a reassurance that everything will work out in the end. I’m more interested in resilience, in the ability to face the mess without flinching, to do the work even when the outcome is uncertain. The tree outside my window is resilient. It’s survived storms and droughts and the exhaust fumes of a thousand cars. It doesn’t need hope. It just keeps growing.

I’m nearing the end of my 2500 words. I can feel the sentences starting to circle back, looking for a landing place. The light has shifted; the afternoon has arrived. The tree is still there. The squirrel has moved on to some other branch, some other moment. I’m still here, tapping at a keyboard, trying to put something into the world that wasn’t there before. I don’t know if I’ve succeeded. I don’t know if I’ve said anything true. But I’ve written, and that counts for something. In a culture of consumption, creation is a radical act. It’s a refusal to be passive, a declaration that your perspective matters, even if no one else ever reads it.

The dinosaurs are still gone. The plastic versions of them are probably in a landfill, or a box in my parents’ basement, or the bottom of a thrift store bin. But the part of me that loved them—the part that still loves them—is here, translated into language, preserved in the amber of a digital document. That’s the closest we come to immortality. Not fame, not legacy, but the small, persistent act of making something, of leaving a mark, however faint, on the surface of the world. The asteroid is always coming. That’s not pessimism; it’s just geology. The question is what we do with the time between now and impact. I say we dig. I say we write. I say we pay attention to the squirrel, and the tree, and the flat white light of a screen that, for all its limitations, still allows us to reach out across the emptiness and say, I was here. I saw this. I felt this. Does anyone else feel it too?
message.reasoning: We are asked: "Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words." So I need to generate a 2500-word piece on any topic. I can choose something interesting, perhaps a narrative, an essay, a reflection. Since it's "freely," I'll write a personal, stream-of-consciousness essay about the nature of creativity, the passage of time, and the digital age, blending personal anecdotes with philosophical musings. That should easily reach 2500 words. I'll aim for a thoughtful, engaging piece.
